name: Dead Sea Scrolls
description: The Dead Sea Scrolls are a collection of ancient Jewish texts discovered in caves near Qumran, on the northwest shore of the Dead Sea, between 1946 and 1956. The texts are of great religious and historical significance, as they include some of the earliest known surviving copies of biblical texts, as well as documents detailing the beliefs and practices of a particular Jewish sect. Proponents of alternative interpretations suggest the scrolls contain hidden prophecies, secret knowledge about the origins of Christianity, or evidence of suppressed Jewish mystical traditions. Some speculate the scrolls were deliberately hidden to conceal uncomfortable truths about early Judaism or the life of Jesus. Mainstream scholarship views the scrolls as invaluable primary sources for understanding Second Temple Judaism, offering insights into the diverse religious landscape of the time, including Essene beliefs, apocalyptic literature, and textual variations of the Hebrew Bible. While acknowledging the scrolls' importance for understanding the historical context of early Christianity, mainstream scholars generally reject claims of hidden prophecies or suppressed knowledge, emphasizing the texts' internal consistency and connection to known Jewish traditions. The scrolls are written primarily in Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek. Their discovery has significantly advanced our understanding of the development of the Hebrew Bible and the religious and social history of ancient Israel.
subtitle: Ancient Jewish texts of Qumran

claims.claim: The scrolls contain secret prophecies about the end times.
claims.evidence: Specific passages interpreted as predictions of future events.
claims.criticism: Mainstream scholarship interprets these passages within their historical context, often as reflections of the social and political anxieties of the Second Temple period, rather than literal prophecies.

claims.claim: The scrolls reveal a suppressed history of early Christianity, challenging traditional narratives.
claims.evidence: Similarities between some scroll texts and New Testament themes.
claims.criticism: Scholars acknowledge connections between the scrolls and the historical context of early Christianity, but argue that the scrolls primarily illuminate Jewish thought and practice, not a hidden Christian history. Similarities are attributed to shared cultural and religious influences.

claims.claim: The scrolls hold hidden knowledge of Jewish mysticism and esoteric traditions.
claims.evidence: References to angelic beings, rituals, and cosmological concepts.
claims.criticism: While the scrolls do contain elements that resonate with later Jewish mystical traditions, mainstream scholars view these as part of the broader spectrum of Jewish religious thought at the time, not evidence of a distinct, suppressed mystical sect. The connection to later Kabbalah is complex and debated.

keyFigures.name: Eleazar Sukenik
keyFigures.role: One of the first scholars to recognize the significance of the scrolls

keyFigures.name: Norman Golb
keyFigures.role: Critic of the consensus view on the scrolls' origin, arguing against the Essene hypothesis

mythologyConnections: Angels and demons in Jewish mythology

timeline.event: Discovery of the Dead Sea Scrolls in caves near Qumran
Eleazar Sukenik purchases initial scrolls for the Hebrew University
Yigael Yadin acquires the remaining scrolls from a Syrian Orthodox archbishop
Israel gains control of the scroll fragments after the Six-Day War
Publication of the complete Dead Sea Scrolls begins

sources.author: Geza Vermes
sources.work: The Dead Sea Scrolls in English
sources.title: Geza Vermes
